seo.title: How to Do Payroll for the First Time in Jamaica: Complete 2026 Setup Guide
seo.description: Step-by-step guide for first-time Jamaican employers: register with TAJ, NIS & NHT, calculate statutory deductions, meet deadlines, and avoid common mistakes.

title: How to Do Payroll for the First Time in Jamaica: Complete 2026 Setup Guide
subtitle: Everything New Jamaican Employers Need to Know — Registration, Calculations, Deadlines, and Compliance

ctaInline.headline: Skip the complexity — let Payroll Jamaica handle it

ctaBottom.headline: Ready to run your first payroll the right way?

html:
Running payroll for the first time in Jamaica is both a legal obligation and a significant administrative undertaking. From the moment you take on your first employee, you are required to register with three separate statutory bodies, deduct and remit four different types of contributions, and file monthly and annual returns — all while keeping records in case of audit. This guide walks you through every step of the process, in the order you need to complete them, with the numbers and deadlines you need for 2026.

Step 1: Register Your Business as an Employer Before the First Payroll

Before you can legally run a payroll, you must be registered as an employer with the relevant statutory bodies. There are three registrations required:

1. Tax Administration Jamaica (TAJ)

TAJ administers PAYE income tax and Education Tax. You need a Tax Registration Number (TRN) for your business. If you registered your business as a company or sole trader, you likely already have a TRN. You must then register specifically as an employer with TAJ so that you are set up to file PAYE and Education Tax returns.

Registration can be done at any TAJ office or through the e-Services portal (etax.gov.jm). You will need your business TRN, company registration documents, and details of your business activities. TAJ will assign you an employer registration number and set up your employer account for monthly return submissions.

2. National Insurance Scheme (NIS)

The NIS office registers employers to contribute to the National Insurance Fund. You will need to register your business and register each employee individually. Each employee should already have a personal NIS number — if a new employee does not have one, they must apply at the NIS office. Registration for new employers is done at the NIS regional office serving your business location. You will receive an employer registration number used on all NIS monthly returns.

3. National Housing Trust (NHT)

NHT registration is required for all employers with formal employees. NHT registration is handled through the NHT office. Similar to NIS, each employee must have an NHT number, and you as the employer will receive an employer identifier for use on monthly contribution schedules. Employees who have not previously registered with NHT must do so promptly — unregistered employees cannot benefit from NHT loans or draw-downs regardless of how long contributions have been made on their behalf.

Important: These registrations should be completed before your first pay date. Running payroll and remitting contributions before registration is technically possible but creates administrative complications when you go to file your first monthly return without a valid employer number.

Step 2: Collect Employee Information and Documentation

Before adding an employee to payroll, you need specific information from them:

  • Full legal name (as it appears on their TRN and NIS records)
  • TRN (Tax Registration Number) — this is the employee's unique tax identifier used on all TAJ returns
  • NIS number — required for NIS monthly contribution schedules
  • NHT number — required for NHT monthly schedules
  • Bank account details — for salary disbursement if you are paying by bank transfer (preferred for record-keeping)
  • Agreed salary or hourly rate, pay frequency, and start date
  • Employment contract — signed before or on the first day of employment

For PAYE purposes, you should also ask each employee whether they have another employment. If an employee has a primary employer who is already applying their full income tax threshold, your organisation may be a secondary employer and must withhold PAYE at the marginal rate on all earnings without applying the threshold. Employees must inform you of their primary employment in writing.

Step 3: Understand the Statutory Deductions You Must Calculate

Jamaica requires four statutory deductions on every payroll, two of which have employer contributions on top of the employee deduction. Here is a summary for 2026:

PAYE (Pay As You Earn Income Tax)

PAYE is withheld from the employee's gross pay and remitted to TAJ. The calculation:

  • Annual income tax threshold: JMD $1,500,096 (employees earning below this annually owe no PAYE)
  • On annual chargeable income JMD $1,500,097 to $6,000,000: 25%
  • On annual chargeable income above JMD $6,000,000: 30%
  • Chargeable income = Gross emoluments − Annual threshold − Employee NIS contributions − Approved pension contributions

NIS (National Insurance Scheme)

  • Employee deduction: 3% of insurable wage (max annual wage JMD $5,000,000)
  • Employer contribution: 3% of insurable wage (same ceiling)
  • Maximum annual contribution each: JMD $150,000

NHT (National Housing Trust)

  • Employee deduction: 2% of gross emoluments (no ceiling)
  • Employer contribution: 3% of gross emoluments (no ceiling)

Education Tax

  • Employee deduction: 2.25% of gross emoluments (no ceiling)
  • Employer contribution: 3.5% of gross emoluments (no ceiling)
  • Remitted to TAJ alongside PAYE

Step 4: Calculate Your First Payroll — A Worked Example

Let's walk through a complete payroll calculation for a single employee on their first month of employment.

Employee details: Monthly gross salary JMD $120,000. This is a primary employment. No approved pension contributions.

Step 4a: Calculate NIS

  • NIS base (gross salary): JMD $120,000
  • Monthly NIS ceiling: JMD $416,667 (JMD $5M ÷ 12) — not reached, so full salary is insurable
  • Employee NIS: JMD $120,000 × 3% = JMD $3,600
  • Employer NIS: JMD $120,000 × 3% = JMD $3,600

Step 4b: Calculate NHT

  • Employee NHT: JMD $120,000 × 2% = JMD $2,400
  • Employer NHT: JMD $120,000 × 3% = JMD $3,600

Step 4c: Calculate Education Tax

  • Employee Education Tax: JMD $120,000 × 2.25% = JMD $2,700
  • Employer Education Tax: JMD $120,000 × 3.5% = JMD $4,200

Step 4d: Calculate PAYE

  • Monthly gross emoluments: JMD $120,000
  • Less employee NIS: JMD $3,600
  • Less monthly income tax threshold: JMD $125,008 (JMD $1,500,096 ÷ 12)
  • Monthly chargeable income: JMD $120,000 − $3,600 − $125,008 = negative (JMD −$8,608)
  • Since chargeable income is negative, PAYE = JMD $0

At JMD $120,000/month, this employee falls below the threshold and owes no PAYE. This is a common scenario for lower-paid employees and illustrates why the threshold is so important.

Step 4e: Calculate Net Pay

  • Gross salary: JMD $120,000
  • Less employee NIS: JMD $3,600
  • Less employee NHT: JMD $2,400
  • Less employee Education Tax: JMD $2,700
  • Less PAYE: JMD $0
  • Net pay: JMD $111,300

Step 4f: Total Employer Cost

  • Gross salary: JMD $120,000
  • Plus employer NIS: JMD $3,600
  • Plus employer NHT: JMD $3,600
  • Plus employer Education Tax: JMD $4,200
  • Total employer payroll cost: JMD $131,400

Step 5: Understand Your Remittance Schedule

Jamaica's payroll statutory contributions must be remitted monthly to the relevant bodies. Missing deadlines results in interest charges and penalties that accumulate quickly.

  • TAJ (PAYE + Education Tax): Due by the 14th of the following month. Submit the Employer's Monthly Return (S01 form) with the payment. TAJ accepts payments via their e-Services portal, major banks, and tax offices.
  • NIS: Due by the 14th of the following month. Submit a monthly contribution schedule showing each employee's insurable wage and contribution amount. Payment can be made at NIS offices or by bank transfer.
  • NHT: Due by the 14th of the following month. Submit the monthly NHT contribution schedule. NHT also accepts online payments through their web portal.

For a business running January payroll, all three remittances plus filing documents are due by February 14. For December payroll, they are due by January 14 of the following year. Build these deadlines into your accounting calendar from day one.

Step 6: Generate Compliant Payslips

Every employee must receive a payslip for each pay period. A compliant Jamaican payslip must show at minimum:

  • Employee name and TRN
  • Pay period dates
  • Gross earnings (broken down by component where applicable: basic salary, overtime, bonuses, allowances)
  • Each statutory deduction listed separately: NIS, NHT, Education Tax, PAYE
  • Any voluntary deductions (e.g., credit union, health insurance)
  • Net pay
  • Year-to-date totals for each deduction

Payslips should be retained by the employer for at least seven years in case of TAJ audit or labour dispute. Electronic payslips are acceptable provided employees can access and download them.

Step 7: Annual Filing Requirements

In addition to monthly remittances, employers must complete two annual filings:

P45 / Annual Employer's Return

By March 31 each year, you must file with TAJ a P45 (Employer's Annual Return) showing each employee's total emoluments for the year and total PAYE deducted. TAJ uses this to reconcile what each employee reported on their personal income tax return (IT01) against what you deducted and remitted.

Employee P45 Certificates

Each employee must receive their individual annual tax certificate showing total annual earnings and PAYE deducted. This is also due by March 31 and is the document employees use when filing their personal IT01 tax return or when applying for a tax refund.

Record Keeping Requirements

TAJ can audit your payroll records for up to seven years back. You must maintain:

  • All payroll calculation worksheets or system records
  • Copies of all monthly S01 returns submitted to TAJ
  • All NIS monthly schedules
  • All NHT monthly schedules
  • Evidence of payment for every monthly remittance (bank receipts, confirmation numbers)
  • All employee payslips
  • Employment contracts
  • Leave records (including sick leave, which can affect statutory sick pay interactions)

Cloud-based payroll systems typically retain all of this automatically. If you are running payroll manually, develop a systematic filing system from the first payroll run.

Common Mistakes Made by First-Time Jamaican Employers

Avoid these errors that catch new employers out most frequently:

  • Registering late with TAJ/NIS/NHT: Contributions are owed from the first day of employment, not from the date of registration. Late registration does not reduce your liability — it just means you cannot file returns promptly, which triggers additional penalties.
  • Using the wrong PAYE threshold for part-time or irregular employees: The threshold is annualised. A part-time employee earning JMD $50,000/month has annualised earnings of JMD $600,000 — well below the threshold — so no PAYE applies. Do not simply compare their monthly pay to the monthly threshold figure without annualising first for any unusual circumstances.
  • Forgetting employer contributions in cash flow planning: Many new employers budget only the employee's gross salary. The employer's NIS (3%), NHT (3%), and Education Tax (3.5%) add approximately 9.5% on top for lower earners. Budget for this from day one.
  • Not tracking NIS year-to-date for higher-paid staff: Once an employee's cumulative insurable wage crosses JMD $5,000,000 for the year, NIS contributions stop. Missing this means over-deducting from employees and over-remitting to NIS — a refund process that is administratively burdensome.
  • Missing the 14th deadline: TAJ, NIS, and NHT all charge interest on late remittances. TAJ's current rate is 16.5% per annum — for a business remitting JMD $100,000/month in PAYE, a 30-day delay costs approximately JMD $1,375 in interest, compounding month after month if not corrected.
  • Not getting employee TRNs before first payroll: TAJ requires employee TRNs on the S01 monthly return. If you don't collect TRNs upfront, you cannot file correctly and must chase employees retrospectively — a common but easily avoided problem.

Should You Use Software or Spreadsheets?

For most new Jamaican employers, dedicated payroll software is strongly recommended even from employee one. The statutory calculations involve multiple interacting variables — the PAYE threshold, the NIS ceiling, two PAYE tax bands, and asymmetric rates across four schemes — and any spreadsheet formula error goes undetected until a TAJ audit or employee complaint surfaces it. Purpose-built Jamaican payroll software maintains all rates automatically, generates compliant remittance documents, and produces payslips in the correct format. The monthly subscription cost is almost always less than the time and penalty cost of managing errors in manual payroll. More importantly, it gives you confidence that your first payroll — and every payroll after it — is correct.

Summary: Your First Payroll Checklist

  • Register with TAJ as an employer (obtain employer TRN/registration)
  • Register with NIS (obtain employer registration number)
  • Register with NHT (obtain employer identifier)
  • Collect TRN, NIS number, NHT number, and bank details from each employee
  • Confirm whether each employee is a primary or secondary employment
  • Calculate NIS, NHT, Education Tax, and PAYE for each employee for the pay period
  • Generate and distribute payslips to all employees
  • Remit PAYE + Education Tax to TAJ with S01 return by the 14th of next month
  • Remit NIS contributions with monthly schedule by the 14th of next month
  • Remit NHT contributions with monthly schedule by the 14th of next month
  • File payroll records and payment confirmations
  • Repeat monthly — and file annual P45 return by March 31

Getting the first payroll right sets the foundation for a compliant and scalable employment operation. Take the time to set it up properly, use the right tools, and build the remittance deadlines into your business calendar from day one.
